sentences.text: Yes.
No, I only can say that when we got back to my house, everything was like, at that particular time, there is-- were the stormtroopers, and the Germans were there to destroy the houses.
And my mother got me and my grandmother and Mrs.
Katz, Gunter's mother and the two boys, and we started walking towards another town.
And on the way there, she ran into a farm.
There were like farmlands around there.
And asked the farmer if she could make a phone call to Herford to my father in the factory, let them know what-- wanted to ask him what to do.
So she got my father.
They called him on the phone.
And my father told him we should stay there.
We were outside, and my mother was in the farm.
She should stay there for a few minutes.
She's-- he's trying to get somebody, to call somebody and get his advice.
He had a Christian man who was in some kind of a office in Herford, which had to do with the veterans of 1914-'18.
So he must have been a friend of my father.
I forgot the name.
And my father called him and called my mother back over there that we should try to get to Herford and go to that particular office.
And he will be there.
My father would be there.
So it took us like at least four, six hours to get there.
First we had to walk to the town where we could get the train and then, et cetera, et cetera.
But anyway, we got to that office.
My father was there.
And he closed off the office and told us not to leave.
He would bring us food, and he will be in contact with somebody in Vlotho.
And when everything is over, he would make sure that we get transportation back to Vlotho.
And that this way my father didn't get arrested because everybody else got arrested.
Gunter's father got arrested, too.
On Kristallnacht?
On Kristallnacht-- all the men, most of them went.
So we came back.
This was on the 10th of November, 1938.
And we were hiding out in this office there.
And the next day, I don't remember exactly, but I believe there was some kind of a car that they took us back to Vlotho, and life continued.
There was no schools after that and no work for anybody.
And it was just a waiting period of time till 1942.
A lot of people tried to get out from Germany still.
They went to Holland, not that it helped them very much.
Sometimes, like I mentioned, that one family who moved to Hanover.
And some other people tried to get to Hanover and made it.
But none of them came back anyway.
They all were arrested way ahead of us and sent away and didn't return.
They didn't survive.
So that's the story till [LAUGHS] 1938.
From '38 to '42 just time--
I had to go to work in Herford-- I remember that-- after the Jewish school was over, because the Jewish school only started in '39 to '40.
And in '40-- in '41 to-- I worked in Herford.
I had to go to work because there were a law that old Jewish girls who had no school-- no more school or anything, had to work and were sent to Berlin at the time.
I don't know if you ever heard of that, the [?
Simmons ?] [? worker. ?] There was already something for the war things.
Simmons. ?]
Yeah, and that was in Berlin.
And my father also, through that man in Herford, got me a job by a seamstress, and I had to work for her.
So I still could go out of town.
I was the only one who went back and forth out of town.
And then the seamstress was, I believe, Jewish, and she was arrested with her father before us.
So that was over.
And my-- I remember my father paying somebody for that-- that I could go to-- to work in Herford and was not sent away.
And what happened is that when those first transports went to Russia, the seamstress was in there with her father.
And then I stayed in Vlotho.
I was just, you know, around.
Hanging around and doing things with my mother and father, whatever.
I don't remember.
And then we were arrested.
Let's go back to that time you were arrested and you were brought by truck--
--trucks.
--to this meeting place.
Right-- in Bielefeld, in the next town.
--an interim place.
--like a collection center.
I really don't know.
I really--
Yes.
First of all, it was crowded.
And it was like then already laying on the floor.
There's, you know, no beds or covers or anything.
And we were there for two, three days, and then they took us to Theresienstadt.
No, it was inside.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH], You know, [NON-ENGLISH] is like for soldiers, the--
Like a barracks, right.
And this was called the [NON-ENGLISH] barracks.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's how I remember it.
Yeah, I think we got bread and soup.
I don't know.
I really don't remember that--
It was bad enough.
I mean, I don't really have [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a recollection of what happened.
Everything was sort of drama, you know, like you didn't know what would happen to you.
Yes.
They told us we were going to Theresienstadt.
I don't know.
I don't know what we anticipated.
We had no idea what would be what, really.
Where they were talking about, all the people and sick people concentrating in Theresienstadt in the ghetto, which to us sounded better than going to Poland to the east.
But even then we didn't know what it meant to go to these, really, I mean, in details what to expect.
Yes, we knew, but don't think it-- anybody really expected what was coming and what had he hit a lot of people already.
Like I had family who went to [?
Russia. ?] I had friends from Vlotho who went to Lublin.
My uncle and my aunt from [?
Krefeld ?] went to Lublin, and I remember us getting postcards from there.
But you still didn't know.
You still had no idea what it really was that was going on.
The postcards-- I remember one card.
There were certain things which I didn't want to eat as a kid and it was now the whole family knew I wouldn't eat this, right?
So my aunt and uncle on the card wrote, [?
Miriam ?] would be very happy if she could sauerkraut again-- if she could eat sauerkraut again over here.
So that meant as-- they had made out with my parents like a word to mention if things were bad.
So this must have been one of the things that I would be happy, even if I could get that to eat.
That's one of the items I never would eat.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah, a code.
They must have made out a code what to hide.
No, no, no.
No idea.
No idea.
There was a camp-- it's [?
Bikar ?] in Lublin that I remember.
It's [?
Bisar ?] or it's [?
Bikar, ?] Lublin.
So it was one of those.
So after-- after a few days of waiting in this transit area--
Yeah.
--in these barracks--
Yeah.
--what happened next?
The next thing they put us on trains.
And we were two days and two nights on the train going to Theresienstadt.
There was hardly any food, nothing to drink.
And the worst thing is to have thirst and have nothing to drink.
That was [INAUDIBLE].
That was bad, and it was in the summer.
And it must have been very hot at the time.
And we were so thirsty.
And the train stopped in Leipzig in Germany before going over the Czech border there.
And it was from the Red Cross, were women walking along to the trains where the soldiers were on the [INAUDIBLE].
So we asked them for water, and they told us they don't give anything to Jews.
The Red Cross, the German Red Cross.
And they have in, you know, in Leipzig and Dresden, they have a very funny way of talking German.
The way they said it, you know, like we always imitated them afterwards.
[LAUGHS]
There were regular passenger trains going to Theresienstadt.
Well, it was very, very overcrowded, you know, like a lot of people in-- in [? those ?] [? things. ?] But there were-- there were seats and things.
I don't really, you know, remember.
In those trains, you-- you had like sides-- walkways with the windows.
So it was very, very crowded in there.
Yes-- yes, they had the SS guards right there.
With--
Right on-- with us on the train.
With guns.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Very little to eat.
I don't remember what we had to eat.
If we took that along, or if they give it to us in Bielefeld to take along.
I have no idea.
I just know there was nothing to drink and that thirst-- you, know like you wanted to drink some water or-- couldn't get anything.
I-- you heard occasional screaming from other train-- wagons, you know, and things like that.
But I don't really-- you know, I don't really recall it.
I really don't know anymore exactly how that ride was from Bielefeld to Theresienstadt.
I remember the other one going to Auschwitz.
I was with my mother and my father and--
--and grandmother.
--my grandmother.
Yeah, we were huddling together somewhere.
And the train stopped.
And at that particular time, the trains didn't go into Theresienstadt.
They went into Bohusovice.
[BACKROUND NOISE] This is where the supposedly the train ended.
And then you had to march into the ghetto.
They marched us into the ghetto.
Oh, yeah, I remember that very well.
A lot--
--a lot, a lot of people.
I don't know how far it was.
I didn't-- I don't know-- at least an hour away from there, and I don't know how long it took us to get there.
We had like [POINTING] what they call a backpack.
Knapsack, something like that.
And I really don't remember much taking along.
Oh, of course.
She hardly could walk anymore for years already.
We had, you know, schlepped her.
We got her there.
I don't know.
I really don't know.
There were so many people on the train, and really don't-- you know, from different cities and everything.
Made it to Theresienstadt.
Theresienstadt looked like, I guess, like a army town at that particular time, because there were like-- I don't know the word, [GERMAN].
It mean garrison, right?
It was a garrison from Maria Theresa, [POINTING] those years.
And they built that in Czechoslovakia from, you know, the Austrian queen or whatever it was.
And there were only-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at one particular time, were only military.
So all the military and the people who lived there were out.
And in all the houses, they were full of people already-- I mean, [POINTING] overcrowded, overcrowded.
And--
No.
No.
German civilians or--
No.
No.
I don't know how they got us from Bohusovice into Theresienstadt.
But I didn't see any civilians at all.
It's one of those things.
It was a large area, and it was surrounded what we called the [NON-ENGLISH], which is [POINTING] like a wall around [? its ?] town, like I said, like a military town type of thing.
It was like a round wall going around it.
Topped with-- on top was grass, right?
[POINTING] And underneath were like cellars.
That was the surrounding of Theresienstadt.
And in the center there was a church.
On each side of the church were two big houses.
One of them I lived later on.
There were all little yellow houses, buildings, you know, like an old European town was how it looked.
My mother, my father, and I got into a house which called the-- for war veterans from '14-'18, you know, the Jewish war veterans who were left.
They made like a circuit area there, where they only put people like this, you know, like where one member of the family was a war veteran.
And my father stayed in one room with about, I don't know how many people.
And my mother and I and my grandmother, we were in another room but in the same building.
We stayed in another room.
On the floor we had no beds.
We had nothing.
This was early-- I mean, for a ghetto, it was really early August '42, because, I think the first Jews from Prague came in '42, in the beginning of '42, maybe late '41.
I don't know.
So we were actually one of the first German Jews who got there-- not the first, but one of the first German Jews.
So we stayed there.
It was terrible.
It was horrendous and very little to eat.
We had to go to a-- a barrack, where they had the kitchens.
And you got like a little metal pot, which they called a [GERMAN].
[LAUGHS] And we got the soup and piece of bread and whatever.
Was it enough?
No, it wasn't enough, but that's what we had.
Oh, yeah.
In Theresienstadt we were very hungry-- very, very hungry.
And my mother befriended somebody.
I don't know if you ever heard of him.
He was very well-known from-- Freddy Hersh, have you heard about him?
I just want to know if you ever heard about him?
Oh.
Yeah.
Well, he was a young man who lived in Prague but originally from Aachen, Germany.
And I don't know how my mother met him or not.
My mother started working as a, you know, like a helper's nurse or something like to help sick people there.
That's how she met him, I believe.
And he was the one who started a [?youth?] school over there and put young children together into the houses surrounded by the church, those two buildings.
And he told my mother that I should not be in the house with [? her. ?] I would be much better off if I would be with the younger children together.
And that's how I got into that home there.
We called it a [GERMAN].
And unfortunately, I took very sick right away.
And I was put in what we called a children's [GERMAN], meaning a children's-- like a hospice, hospital type of thing.
Which I stayed from September '42 till April '43.
Very long time.
I had rheumatic fever.
I was very sick.
I couldn't move around.
I was practically paralyzed.
And I was very fortunate in one way.
They had the Jewish doctors from [INAUDIBLE] and Prague and they really saved my life.
That's another story.
They had medications, which people had on them when they got to Theresienstadt, and it was taken away from everybody.
And from that, they made like a [NON-ENGLISH] center, like a drug center.
And they had a Jewish man who was leading this whole thing and gave out the medication when it was needed, all right?
It turned out if he could have been alive after the war, he would have been my father-in-law.
And my mother had to go from where she worked to this particular place to get the medication.
She worked, in matter of fact, for people with TB, because she had the experience through my father, and there was like a little hospital.
Not a hospital-- but, you know, like a-- a room area where they only had TB patients.
And that's where my mother worked, and she was sent from there to this big [INAUDIBLE] place.
And Mr.
Gottesman, who was my father-in-law, was the one who gave out the medication.
And when she told him that I was so sick-- [INAUDIBLE] she must have been crying, whatever-- that I was so sick and that I'm hospitalized, he gave her I don't know what you-- [? glucosa. ?]
You know what [? glucosa ?] is?
It's like a-- sugar from-- from grapes-- grape sugar.
And they made liquids out of it, and people got it as-- I don't know for what illnesses-- as injections.
And he gave my mother those whenever she went there and told her when she sees me to give it to me, to break it open, and let me drink it-- that I could drink it so it would give me energy.
That's how we got into medication.
Now, there was very little medication for what I needed.
And I was very lucky that one of the doctors [INAUDIBLE] Dr.
Schwartz, who didn't survive it either, a young doctor, he was friends with a Czech-- [?
Czechnik. ?] A [?
Czechnik ?] is a a Czech policeman who worked for the Germans in-- in the ghetto.
And this man would go back every week or every second week took Prague on a furlough.
And this doctor told him to bring medication from Prague, if he could bring this and this medication.
And I got that, and it really saved my life.
It was injections of bee poison, which is for-- specialized for rheumatoid fever at the time.
So this is how I made it.
[LAUGHS] [INAUDIBLE].
My father, believe it or not, I don't know what he was really working.
I think he work-- worked in the basement where they sorted potatoes.
So he would steal potatoes, cook them, and bring them to the hospital to me.
[LAUGHS] Then my grandmother was very sick.
My mother took care till the last minute of her.
And she died in February '43-- 1943.
I still was in the hospital at that time, so they came to tell me.
[SIGH] I think she died of old age, number one, and hunger, and not being in a house, not being home, and not having her [POINTS] comfort-- that way.
And it--
It was very funny.
I dreamt two days before-- two nights before my grandmother died, I dreamt that my mother-- grandmother died.
And when my mother and father came to tell me, I told them that I knew already.
Now I break up.
[CRYING] That's it-- one of those psychic things I think can happen to anybody in life.
No, it wasn't.
It really wasn't.
I knew [? it ?] already.
It was amazing.
It was amazing what these people did for me.
Because if not for them, I-- I would have died a long time before even anything else happened there.
They really did-- [BACKROUND NOISE] there were three doctors.
Yeah.
There were three doctors, who individually took care of me.
[BACKROUND NOISE] There were two doctors-- actually all three of them were from Czechoslovakia.
One was Dr.
Schwartz from [INAUDIBLE], and one was Dr.
Haiek from [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Prague, and one was Dr.
Zonzhavic, which was a woman, from-- also from [INAUDIBLE].
And between the three of them, they tried everything and anything to take care of me and the other children who were there.
A lot of children died right next to me.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes, I saw children die then already.
I remember waking up in the morning, and the bed was-- the bed-- the cot was empty.
And I was told oh, died during the night.
It didn't mean anything.
I mean, it was shocking for us children.
But after a while, it didn't really mean anything anymore.
Then I had one experience in-- it was the early part of 1943.
There was a transport going of very sick children to Auschwitz then already.
We didn't know about Auschwitz, but we knew there was a transport going.
And I was in there.
I was in that transport already.
And the doctors hid me.
They had like a type of a closet.
And they opened up the closet, put the cot in there, took the door out.
No, they didn't put the door on.
They left the door in and put a sign on the door-- typhoid, typhoid patient.
So when they came to pick me up, you know, like with the cot to pick me up, they said, oh, this one has typhoid?
You know like that.
So the doctor said, look, don't even take her along.
She wouldn't even make it to the train station.
That's how they saved my life.
That was in 1943.
I think it must have been in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I believe it was in February-- January, February-- in winter '43.
They sent me back to the home, which in between got very, very overcrowded because a lot of children came from Germany, from Austria, et cetera.
And I got back there.
And little by little, when I felt better, I started to work, like everybody else.
And that was it.
The sanitary were very bad, but we tried to make the best out of it.
I have to say that I don't where we-- where we found it.
I mean, we stayed in-- in line to go to the toilets.
And in our room, we had a girl who was in charge of the showers, what we called showers, OK?
And being that she was in our room, she naturally gave us extra time to do things and when there was water and when there was heat, and you survived.
You did things.
I only remember, I think, once a day.
I don't know if we had in the morning, in the noon, or if we had in the morning and-- in morning and at noon, I think.
We had a piece of bread and what they called coffee or whatever.
And then in Theresienstadt they had bread, and they had food we called [GERMAN].
It was like a cake-type-- type of thing.
Instead of bread, you could get that.
And what else was it?
Soups.
And then later on, I worked in-- outside the ghetto in the gardens and in the fields, so we stole things and tried to feed us that way.
What type of work were you doing after you got better?
First I was working in-- they had like a-- a-- a repair shop for optical-- like glasses, right, and things like that.
And I would-- was working in there.
It was a Dr.
Yelinek ?] I worked for.
He was an ops-- what do you call it, a optical doctor?
And the-- optometrist, right?
And after that, I had just actually two jobs I had in ghetto was what we called the [GERMAN] out at the gardens, in the fields.
It was hard work.
It was very hard work.
But everybody wanted to work like that because there was always a chance to get something to eat somewhere from the field, especially corn.
We had what they call in Czech [CZECH].
[CZECH] is corn, the corn fields and et cetera.
[SIGHS] We had a Jewish girl who took care of us [? outside ?] and some man.
And the SS was around.
But you make a go of it, you know.
I wasn't, not there.
I wasn't.
I mean, I had certain experiences there because the one who took care of us out there had a way of telling [? us. ?] We were in a field right near the [?
Ohre, ?] which a river in Czechoslovakia.
And they were like big green bushes there.
And then when she saw that, some of the girls got very tired or so.
She said, oh, lay down and sleep for a little while, or rest for a little while.

So my one experience was there that near Theresienstadt was what they called the [GERMAN], the small-- it was clearly the concentration camp, not the ghetto, and-- which was very bad, which we didn't even know how bad it was at that time already.
OK, all right.
OK.
Ok.
